meta.title: Box 3 explained: what it is and how it works
meta.description: A clear explanation of Box 3 tax in the Netherlands: deemed return, tax-free allowance, the Christmas ruling and what changes in 2027. For anyone with savings, investments or a second property.
meta.keywords: box 3 explained, box 3 tax netherlands, deemed return box 3, tax-free capital 2025, box 3 how does it work

hero.title: Box 3 explained
hero.subtitle: What it is, how it works, and why it has been in court for years.

threeBoxes.title: Three boxes, one system
threeBoxes.p1: In the Netherlands, income tax is divided into three boxes. Box 1 covers income from work and your primary home, which for most people is the largest part of their tax return. Box 2 applies to people with a substantial interest in a private company. And Box 3 covers all remaining assets: savings, investments, a second property, rental properties.
threeBoxes.p2: Most people only really encounter Box 3 once they build up assets above the tax-free threshold. Below that point, you won't notice it at all.

fictiveReturn.title: Deemed return: the core concept
fictiveReturn.p1: What makes Box 3 unusual is that the Dutch tax authority does not look at what you actually earned. Instead they apply a deemed return: a percentage you are assumed to have made, regardless of what the markets did or how much interest your bank paid.
fictiveReturn.p2: You then pay 36% tax on that deemed return. Not on your assets themselves, but on the fictitiously calculated return on them.
fictiveReturn.p3: In practice: if you have €200,000 in investments, the tax authority assumes you earned 6.04%, which comes to €12,080. You pay 36% on that, totalling €4,348.80 in tax. Whether you actually made that return does not matter (for now).

rates.title: The 2025 rates
rates.intro: The deemed return is split per category:

items.label: Bank savings
items.note: low, due to low savings rates

items.label: Investments & property
items.note: higher, assumed to carry more risk

rates.taxNote: You pay 36% tax on the total deemed return. There is also a tax-free allowance of €57,000 per person (€114,000 for fiscal partners). If you are below that, you pay nothing.

courtCases.title: Why there have been so many court cases
courtCases.p1: The old Box 3 system (before 2023) used one fixed deemed return for everything, around 4% a few years ago. The problem: if your savings only earned 0.1% interest but you were taxed on a 4% deemed return, you were effectively paying more than you actually earned.
courtCases.p2: The Supreme Court ruled in December 2021 that this was incompatible with property rights under the ECHR and declared the system unconstitutional. This became known as the Christmas ruling.
courtCases.p3: Taxpayers who had filed a timely objection were entitled to remediation. Those who had not filed an objection were initially left out, and legal proceedings on that point are still ongoing.

currentSystem.title: The current system and what comes next
currentSystem.p1: Since 2023 a bridging scheme has applied. The tax authority now distinguishes between asset categories, hence the separate rates for savings, investments, and debts. It is still a deemed-return system, but fairer than before.
currentSystem.p2: The government has chosen accrual taxation (aanwasbelasting) as the basis for the new system: you pay annually on the actual increase in value of your assets, not on a fictitious percentage. Exception: real estate and shares in startups will be taxed under a profit tax, payable at the moment of sale. The start date is 1 January 2028.
currentSystem.p3: Until then the bridging scheme remains in force. The takeaway: understand the system, calculate your tax, and make sure you are not paying more than necessary.
